explanation_md: `Status::warn` is the second enumerator of the scoped enum, so it has the value 1. The struct `Result<int>` instantiates with a scoped-enum member and an `int` member; the `static_cast<int>(r.s)` prints `1`, and `r.value` is the literal `42`.

options.a: `Player p{ .x = 0, .y = 0, .t = Team::red };` uses designated initializers — valid in C++20.
options.b: `Player p{ 0, 0, red };` is valid because unscoped names from `Team` enter the surrounding scope.
options.c: `Player p = { 0, 0 };` initializes `t` to a garbage value because aggregates do not zero unspecified members.
options.d: Defining `Player` like this is invalid because a struct cannot mix fundamental types and enum types.

explanation_md: Designated initializers (C++20) name members explicitly, which is the readable choice when several fields have similar types. Option (b) is wrong because `Team::red` is scoped and does not appear bare. Option (c) is wrong: aggregate members left out of the brace list are value-initialized (zero for fundamental types, the enum's zero state for `Team`). Option (d) is simply false — structs can hold members of any type.

options.a: The program prints `11` — there is no bug.
options.b: `bump` takes its parameter by value, so it modifies a copy and the original `b.value` is still 10 when printed.
options.c: Class templates cannot have member values that are modified.
options.d: `Box<int>` and `Box` are different types and the call fails to compile.

explanation_md: Passing a struct (templated or not) by value copies it into the parameter. `bump` then modifies its local copy. The caller's `b` is untouched, so the program prints `10`. To actually mutate the caller's object the parameter must be `Box<int>&`. Option (d) is wrong: with CTAD or explicit `Box<int>`, the call type-checks; the issue is purely the value-vs-reference choice.

Default member initializers fill in for any field not given a value. For `a{}` both members default — `x` to 0, `y` to 5. For `b{ .x = 2 }`, `x` is 2 and `y` keeps its default of 5. The four digits are printed without separators inside each pair, giving `05 25`.

options.a: Because templates only support a single instantiation per program.
options.b: Because `Pair<int>` and `Pair<long>` are different, unrelated types — different instantiations of the same template do not implicitly convert to one another.
options.c: Because `long` and `int` are the same size on most platforms, so the compiler refuses the redundant copy.
options.d: Because aggregate initialization only allows literal initializers.

explanation_md: Two instantiations of a class template are distinct types in the type system, even when the underlying types (`int` and `long`) freely convert in arithmetic expressions. The compiler treats `Pair<int>` and `Pair<long>` as unrelated and refuses the assignment, just as it would refuse to assign a `std::string` to a `std::vector`.
